title: Sourdough Focaccia Recipe with Herbs
meta_description: Master a crispy, airy sourdough focaccia recipe with herbs. Learn the best techniques and essential tools for the perfect golden-brown crumb.
html:
There is something uniquely satisfying about the contrast of a salty, olive-oil-drenched crust and a soft, bubbly interior. While sourdough loaves often require hours of tension-building and precise shaping, focaccia is the relaxed cousin of the bread world. This sourdough focaccia recipe focuses on high hydration and a long cold ferment to get those signature large air pockets without the stress of a formal knead.

Essential Tools for Your Sourdough Focaccia Recipe

Before diving into the ingredients, let's talk gear. You don't need a commercial bakery, but a few specific tools make the process significantly easier. Mixing high-hydration dough by hand can be messy; that's where a Danish dough whisk comes in. It cuts through thick batter and incorporates flour much faster than a standard spoon.

To keep your workspace clean and your dough handled properly, I highly recommend the 3-in-1 Sourdough Whisk Set (Whisk, Scraper, Lame). The dough scraper is essential for transferring the sticky focaccia batter into your baking pan, and while you won't need the scoring lame for this specific flatbread, it's a must-have for when you transition back to sourdough boules.

Ingredients You'll Need

  • 100g active, bubbly sourdough starter
  • 375g warm water
  • 500g bread flour (high protein is key for structure)
  • 10g fine sea salt
  • 1/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil (plus more for the pan)
  • Fresh rosemary, thyme, and flaky sea salt for topping

Step-by-Step Sourdough Focaccia Instructions

Start by whisking your active starter and warm water together in a large bowl. Use the Danish dough whisk from the 3-in-1 Sourdough Whisk Set to ensure the starter is fully dissolved. Stir in the bread flour and salt until a shaggy dough forms. Don't overwork it at this stage; you just want no dry flour remaining.

Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and let it rest for 30 minutes. This "autolyse" period allows the flour to hydrate, making the dough easier to handle. Instead of kneading, perform a series of "stretch and folds." Grab the edge of the dough, pull it upward, and fold it over itself. Rotate the bowl and repeat this four times every 30 minutes for two hours.

For the best flavor, place the dough in a greased container and let it ferment in the refrigerator overnight. This cold proof develops the complex, tangy flavor sourdough is known for. The next day, generously oil a 9x13 inch baking pan and transfer the dough using your dough scraper to avoid degassing the bubbles.

The Magic of the Dimpling Process

Once the dough has relaxed in the pan and filled the corners, preheat your oven to 425°F (220°C). This is the most rewarding part of the sourdough focaccia recipe: the dimpling. Pour a generous amount of olive oil over the top of the dough.

Press your fingertips straight down into the dough to create deep craters. You should feel the air bubbles popping beneath your fingers. This creates the perfect reservoirs for the olive oil and herbs to pool in. Scatter your fresh rosemary and thyme across the top and finish with a heavy sprinkle of flaky sea salt.

Baking and Cooling for Maximum Crunch

Slide the pan into the center rack of the oven. Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the top is golden brown and the edges are crisp. If you want an extra-crunchy bottom, you can bake the focaccia on the lowest rack for the final five minutes.

The hardest part is the wait. Let the focaccia cool in the pan for about 10 minutes, then transfer it to a wire cooling rack immediately. If you leave it in the pan too long, the bottom can steam and lose its crispness. Slice into squares and serve warm with a side of balsamic vinegar and olive oil for dipping.

FAQ

Can I use all-purpose flour instead of bread flour?

Yes, you can, but your focaccia will have a slightly softer structure and fewer large bubbles. Bread flour has more protein, which creates the strong gluten network needed to trap those big air pockets during fermentation.

What if my dough isn't bubbling after the overnight proof?

Check your kitchen temperature. If your house is cold, the dough may need a few extra hours at room temperature before baking. Ensure your starter was fully active and peaked before you began the mix.

How do I store leftover sourdough focaccia?

Store leftovers in an airtight container at room temperature for up to two days. To revive the crunch, pop a slice in a toaster oven or a dry skillet over medium heat for 2-3 minutes. Avoid the microwave, as it will make the bread chewy.
